review_summary: Transcript is sufficient for reinforcement questions about skip-gram versus CBOW, naive softmax, negative sampling, sigmoid scoring, and the 3/4 unigram distribution. The mechanics are described explicitly, so no separate clip is needed.

question_bank.question: Why does word2vec keep separate center and outside vectors in the explanation here?
choices: To make the model multilingual
To avoid a quadratic self-term that makes the math messier
To eliminate the need for normalization
To guarantee antonyms are opposites
question_bank.explanation: If the same vector were reused in both roles, the center word can reappear in the normalization and create a quadratic self-term. Separate vectors keep the derivation simpler.

question_bank.question: Which pairing correctly matches the two classic word2vec structures?
choices: Skip-gram predicts the center word from context; CBOW predicts context from the center word
Skip-gram predicts surrounding words from the center word; CBOW predicts the center word from context
Both skip-gram and CBOW predict only part-of-speech tags
Both skip-gram and CBOW require full-batch training
question_bank.explanation: Skip-gram starts from the center word and predicts words around it, whereas CBOW pools the context and predicts the center word.

transcript_quotes: tried to predict the words around it
predict the center word from the context

question_bank.question: Why is naive softmax expensive for large vocabularies?
choices: It requires one matrix inverse per token
It sums over every word in the vocabulary in the denominator
It can only run on CPUs
It duplicates each context window twice
question_bank.explanation: Naive softmax requires scoring all vocabulary items for each training example, which becomes expensive when the vocabulary is very large.

question_bank.question: What is the main idea of negative sampling?
choices: Replace word vectors with one-hot vectors
Train the model to score the true context word high and a few sampled non-context words low
Normalize over every possible context word more accurately
Use only the rarest words as positive examples
question_bank.explanation: Negative sampling turns the update into a small set of binary discrimination problems: the real context word should look plausible, while sampled noise words should not.

question_bank.question: What effect does drawing negatives from unigram probability to the 3/4 power have?
choices: It makes all words equally likely to be sampled
It fully removes frequent words from sampling
It partially flattens the frequency distribution while still favoring common words
It samples only words already in the current context
question_bank.explanation: Raising probabilities to the 3/4 power reduces the dominance of the very most frequent words without making the distribution uniform.
